block_01: The last week of the year 2020 is finally here which means it’s time to take stock with the IQ Trending annual report! As we stand at the threshold of the New Year 2021, let's do the past trading year in review and see which trading assets were beating the odds throughout 2020.
block_04: The leading electric vehicles manufacturer headed by Elon Musk left no chance for other companies with publicly traded stocks to join today’s hit-list.  Since the beginning of 2020, Tesla’s share price has surged more than 600%, thus securing TSLA a place in the ETF S&P 500 along with giants like Apple and Google, and making Elon Musk the second richest person on the planet.
block_08: The year 2020 hit the Turkish economy especially hard. By the end of the year, a debt crisis in the country multiplied by the world pandemic led to Turkish Lira devaluation by almost 30% against the US Dollar, making USD/TRY the most volatile currency pair of the year.
block_12: The Father of crypto finally got its throne back. Despite all the skepticism around cryptocurrencies, it’s impossible to overestimate Bitcoin’s value on a global financial scale. Over the past year, BTC price has risen by more than 200%, surpassing its 2017 historical high and reaching a new maximum of $25,000. What will be next? Only Satoshi knows…
block_16: Gold is the most tradable precious metal and is widely viewed as a hedge against inflation and a safe haven asset during turbulent times for the world economy.
block_17: Rising coronavirus cases during the world pandemic and currencies’ depreciation created an unprecedented demand for Gold (including gold-backed ETFs). By August 2020, the commodity price hit a historical high of $2000 benefiting against incentives taken by central banks.
block_22: In March 2020, the US Federal Reserve announced it would cut its target interest rate to the lowest 0,25% in order to support the economy during the coronavirus pandemic.
block_23: The Fed interest rate is a critical indicator for the world economy since it affects all major interest rates, including inflation rate and consumers’ loans and credit cards.
